meta.title: TechPulse
meta.tagline: Tech & AI news, hot takes, and who's saying what — from the builders you follow
meta.note: Built from your X Following timeline, filtered to tech/AI builders by profile. Click any story to see every take and what different people are saying.

stories.title: Fable Access Drama: Coming Back, But Is It Actually Good?
stories.summary: Anthropic's flagship 'Fable' model dominated timelines again — some builders call it agent-aware magic for orchestrating subagents and swarms, while others are counting hallucinations, complaining it's gotten dumber, and still reaching for GPT-5.5 instead. Access changes kicking in after July 7 have people bracing for a return to subscription limits.

takes.name: Matt Pocock
takes.text:
10 minutes using Fable, 2 factuality-based hallucinations already

Never trust an LLM

takes.name: Can Vardar
takes.text: is it just me or did fable get way dumber?

takes.name: Steve Ruiz
takes.text:
"no Linear MCP in this session, so I built a small CLI helper that reuses your cached Linear OAuth token (from the tldraw repo's mcp-remote setup) to call Linear's MCP API directly. Agents use it to read their issue and post a progress comment."

jfc fable just ask me to log in

takes.name: Theo - t3.gg
takes.text:
Fable’s intuition for iOS development is significantly worse than other areas I’ve used it in.

Great at infra, databases, web and more, just easily confused about how mobile apps work for some reason

takes.name: Mirko Monti
takes.text:
Good news! Even if there is no timing 

Anthropic will give us back on subscription Fable in the future after 7 July

takes.name: Layton Gott
takes.text: Codex July 8th trying to do everything without Fable: https://t.co/q4jfZ2lFD2

takes.name: Julius
takes.text: The amount of world knowledge in Fable is insane. It’s navigating iOS simulators without any skills way better than codex with the OpenAI plugin 🤯

takes.name: Kent C. Dodds 🏹
takes.text: Fable 5 orchestrating a swarm of subagents (I'm using Composer 2.5 for those with @cursor_ai) is just magic. Finding the critical path and breaking things up as much as reasonable for parallelization is so great. 🤌

takes.name: Riley Brown
takes.text: I used Fable 5 to play as Anthropic in the race to super intelligence. ft @vishal_dubey, the legend. https://t.co/VYZZamCGRx

takes.name: Kevin Kern
takes.text: Fable is such an agent-aware model. https://t.co/X6nCaIU4G7

takes.name: vimtor
takes.text:
can i say something?

i like gpt-5.5 better

takes.name: Can Vardar
takes.text: gpt-5.5 still feels better than fable 5 in both UX and speed, despite anthropic claiming fable 5 is the superior model

takes.name: Theo - t3.gg
takes.text:
btw this same benchmark puts Sonnet 5, GLM 5.2, and Nemotron 3 Ultra all over Fable 5

Very trustworthy 🙃 https://t.co/Shq5atEG6I

takes.name: Mirko Monti
takes.text:
Tried Claude Fable 5?

It feels like a proper senior engineer compared to Opus 4.8 or GPT-5.5.

Example: Solved a tricky new feature on my side project that I was stuck on for days with Opus. One prompt and it just… got it.
Huge step up for complex, real work. 

What’s been your

takes.name: Dev Ed
takes.text: I was on 99% of my current session limit using Fable 5, quickly loaded in $20 and this happened https://t.co/IJVX1nosOG

stories.title: The "Should You Read AI-Written Code" Debate Goes Viral
stories.summary: Theo's mega-thread asking how much better models need to get before you stop reading the code exploded into a timeline-wide debate. Some compare it to trusting compilers over assembly; others (Uncle Bob included) warn this is exactly how slop quietly takes over codebases.

takes.name: Theo - t3.gg
takes.text: How much better do the models have to get before you'll stop reading the code?

takes.name: Theo - t3.gg
takes.text:
You should be reviewing a MUCH smaller % of your code today than you were 5 years ago.

If your code is so important it needs every line verified, you better be writing a LOT of slop that verifies it too. https://t.co/v5qVqpUxP5

takes.name: Theo - t3.gg
takes.text: At this point I’m genuinely convinced most of you would have kept reading the assembly code after C got popular

takes.name: dax
takes.text: guys stop trying to argue with the "code doesn't matter" posts and just ask them if they're so ahead of the game why aren't they richer

takes.name: dax
takes.text:
prior to ai coding people would bitch about other people's code to signal they have some highly refined taste

now people bitch about ai's code

no one good has ever been bitching guys

takes.name: Matt Pocock
takes.text:
The "should you read code" debate is dumb because the real decision isn't binary, it's a scale:

1. Reading every line of every diff
2. Scanning every diff, reviewing important lines
3. Ignoring diffs but understanding the 'why' of every PR
4. Spot checking PR's instead of

takes.name: Can Vardar
takes.text: the more i rely on ai without reading what it writes the less i understand my own project and the less i understand my own project the harder it becomes to make good decisions and keep the codebase healthy

takes.name: Kent C. Dodds 🏹
takes.text: Yep, been working this way as well. I'm planning a video to talk about how I review system changes etc.

takes.name: Tim Sweeney
takes.text: @theo Moving from assembly language to compilers, there was a 24 month window where it mattered.

takes.name: Lee Robinson
takes.text:
Are current LLMs incompatible with great creative writing?

I can't tell if it's cope or not, but it seems like even with the best models, I still can't get them to write like humans would.

For coding, there is a verifiable reward like it compiling or tests passing. But for

takes.name: Micky
takes.text:
Most people who complain about slop code being generated by AI are the same ones that write slop themselves

Very few can actually complain lol

takes.name: Uncle Bob Martin
takes.text:
@ThePrimeagen For the last fifty years it’s been about preventing the slop from overflowing. It was about then that we stopped looking at the true state of the system. We simply assumed that whatever the state of the system was, at the binary level, it was probably good enough.

Believe me,

stories.title: Everyone's Building Their Own Agent Orchestration Playbook
stories.summary: From Cursor cloud agents managing other cloud agents, to Wayfinder PRD sessions, model-routing tools, and skills libraries, builders keep converging on the same idea: orchestrating swarms of specialized agents beats one big prompt.

takes.name: Kent C. Dodds 🏹
takes.text: Result of this was 6 individual @cursor_ai cloud agents orchestrated by the original cloud agent which took learnings from some agents and shared it with the others. It was brilliant

takes.name: Kent C. Dodds 🏹
takes.text:
This is fable orchestrating composer 2.5 to migrate my website to Cloudflare from fly. 

https://t.co/A20psm49bk

takes.name: Layton Gott
takes.text:
Skills have a HUGE impact in AI coding right now…

And they're free.

A skill is just a markdown file with a dialed in prompt essentially. 

Your agent reads it every time it's called, so you get the same behavior every time.

Installing one takes a sentence. Paste the GitHub

takes.name: Layton Gott
takes.text:
Your AI agent gets dumber and dumber over time…

It starts strong. 

Then halfway through a long task it forgets stuff, repeats itself, and ignores what you told it.

The cause is simple: context window.

The fuller that window gets, the dumber the model gets. 

There's also a

takes.name: Matt Pocock
takes.text:
This is a PRD based on a multi-day /wayfinder session

Look how detailed it is

Look how every assertion is linked back to the session where it was decided

Secondary source -> Primary source

Beautiful

https://t.co/AMVKBNJq6X

takes.name: Matt Pocock
takes.text:
I fucking love prototyping

Wayfinder killing it again with a modal I can open anywhere to edit the text of my courses with AI https://t.co/xMuCwGWPd8

takes.name: Kevin Kern
takes.text:
adding something new to planr: model routing.

declare once which model handles which work. one plans and judges, others implement, each where it‘s strongest (e.g gpt5.5 for backend and opus 4.8 for frontend)

works with codex, claude code, cursor + any MCP client.

planr is the https://t.co/8XHq6W8Sgy

takes.name: Mirko Monti
takes.text:
What's your current workflow? 

I launch parallel agents in cloud with GUI and I do "micor" local dev and debugging with cli

takes.name: Steve Ruiz
takes.text:
"no Linear MCP in this session, so I built a small CLI helper that reuses your cached Linear OAuth token (from the tldraw repo's mcp-remote setup) to call Linear's MCP API directly. Agents use it to read their issue and post a progress comment."

jfc fable just ask me to log in

takes.name: dex
takes.text: this is the way

stories.title: The Cheap Model Race: Kimi K2.7 Lands in Copilot, Sonnet 5 Gets Love
stories.summary: Kimi K2.7 Code became the first open-weight model in the GitHub Copilot picker, Kimi Code added Superpowers support, and builders are re-ranking the whole model hierarchy now that Sonnet 5 behaves like an old Opus and categorizing models "got harder."

takes.name: GitHub
takes.text:
Kimi K2.7 Code is the first open-weight model you can select in the GitHub Copilot model picker. What does that mean for you?

@burkeholland explains how this low-cost, high-performance model gives you more choice and flexibility in your workflow. ▶️ https://t.co/rxkmT2cABP

takes.name: Layton Gott
takes.text:
I never thought I'd love a Sonnet model so much...

Yet here I am using Sonnet 5 all the time for everyday tasks and simple UI/UX work.

This model is genuinely so nice for everyday tasks:

• It's plenty smart
• It's fast
• It doesn't overthink and is super direct
• It's

takes.name: Matt Pocock
takes.text:
Feels like categorising models got harder recently

I used to put models in the bucket of Opus-like, Sonnet-like, or Haiku-like.

But now we have Fable. Now Sonnet 5 behaves like Opus. Is GLM 5.2 Opus-like, or Sonnet 5-like?

So, I'm asking. How are you evaluating models?

takes.name: Kimi Developers
takes.text:
Superpowers now officially supports Kimi Code.🤝

If you're already using the Superpowers + Kimi Code combo, we'd love to hear your best practices.🌟

https://t.co/Zl2PotQ4u1 https://t.co/nH43jEnRKl

takes.name: Can Vardar
takes.text: calling it now: the grok comeback is going to catch everyone off guard

takes.name: Mirko Monti

stories.title: AI Video and Design Tooling Keeps Getting More Real
stories.summary: Higgsfield brought Gemini Omni Flash and Seed Audio into Premiere Pro and DaVinci Resolve, a new framework called egaki lets agents write entire videos in MDX, and designers are using AI to generate clickable briefs instead of walls of text.

takes.name: Higgsfield AI 🧩
takes.text: AI VFX powered by Gemini Omni Flash. https://t.co/OSTeweDsdq

takes.name: Higgsfield AI 🧩
takes.text:
Gemini Omni Flash and Seed Audio 1.0 hit Adobe Premiere Pro and DaVinci Resolve Studio via Higgsfield Plugins.

Clean a background with a prompt, generate multi-shots, and add motion graphics in the timeline.

Change a voice, narrate from text, or dub a clip into 18 languages. https://t.co/Nc5RO1iMWv

takes.name: Tommy (in SF from July 6)
takes.text:
introducing egaki

a framework to make videos with mdx. for agents & humans

the video below was entirely written by Fable 5. I didn't touch a video editor or code once https://t.co/3DXsgxXqYr

takes.name: Ridd 🤿
takes.text:
my new favorite workflow: having AI generate clickable briefs

for example I had it research every piece of metadata I could use for a design and spin it up as an HTML page

so instead of a wall of text I can "see" each concept

but the cool part is I can just scroll through and https://t.co/28AHe8bAzq

takes.name: Praveen Kumar
takes.text: Dither + ASCII + Motion = https://t.co/u0YNjk6kk8

stories.title: Vercel Ships a Wave of Agent-Friendly Infra
stories.summary: Vercel pushed Sandbox support for Docker and FUSE-based filesystems, shipped Agent Runs on MCP/CLI for introspecting past agent runs, and Rauchg animated a chart of trillions of tokens flowing through the AI Gateway.

takes.name: Guillermo Rauch
takes.text:
This is so powerful. ▲ Sandbox can now run 𝚍𝚘𝚌𝚔𝚎𝚛 and 𝚏𝚞𝚜𝚎, unconstrained.

MicroVMs are the gift that keeps on giving. Instant boots, unconstrained runtimes. Very happy we bet on it as the foundation for 🌊 Fluid compute.

S3-backed filesystem for agents in 10LOC ↓

takes.name: Guillermo Rauch
takes.text:
Agentic self-improvement. 

Give your agent the ability to introspect its past runs, spot inefficiencies, errors, redundant tool calls, and produce new prompts and skills.

That’s why agent observability is built-in when you deploy https://t.co/99eEa13mZ3 to Vercel.

takes.name: Guillermo Rauch
takes.text:
🏁 I animated the token 💰 spend race, from ~lifetime Vercel AI Gateway usage, which aggregates trillions of tokens from millions of developers a month.

Fascinating to see the fluctuations among the labs, Anthropic's dominance, and the rise of open weight AI. https://t.co/iX8oRA4SBP

takes.name: Vercel Developers
Vercel Developers
takes.text:
Sandbox now supports FUSE-based filesystems.

▪︎ Mount S3 buckets and network filesystems
▪︎ Run CLI tools against remote sources
▪︎ Share state across Sandboxes

https://t.co/mTlXIfHeXB

takes.name: Evil Rabbit
takes.text: ▲ Geist Pixel is now available on Google Fonts. https://t.co/4u2HhJynRJ

stories.title: Is AI Design Tooling Finally Beating Figma?
stories.summary: Framer's Agent mode for mobile layouts and Figmotion demos have builders wondering out loud if AI tools have leapfrogged Figma for parts of the design workflow.

takes.name: Can Vardar
takes.text: i still can’t believe we got software that’s actually better than figma

takes.name: Abraham John 🦄🦓
takes.text:
Designers, stop manually designing your website for mobile devices.

→ Instead, go to @framer 
→ Prepare your design on desktop only.
→ Select your layout and switch to Agent.
→ And enter the prompt below
→ Get a mobile and tablet view instantly
→ Tweak it inside the chat https://t.co/msjoyNDUnU

takes.name: uixamjad
takes.text: Nice play with Figmotion 🫣🥳 @BrettFromDJ   https://t.co/F8uY4R7bwQ

stories.title: Git Worktrees Go Mainstream in the Agent Coding Era
stories.summary: Git worktrees, around since 2015, are having a moment as AI-era parallel agent work makes them feel essential — GitHub explained why, while builders shared their own worktree-based workflows for reviewing and running agents in parallel.

takes.name: GitHub
matt palmer
takes.text: What are git worktrees in @conductor_build and why use them? https://t.co/bCJsmptiZ9

takes.name: trash
takes.text:
me: i wish i could review big prs without the browser crashing

github: here’s a cd you can put your repo on

me: https://t.co/YglTEBjQoS

stories.title: Garry Tan's Housing Push: Build More, Defund the NIMBYs
stories.summary: Y Combinator president Garry Tan spent the window hammering a consistent message: SF needs to build housing fast, data centers are financing vehicles that drag whole industries forward, and NIMBYs are blocking real solutions with performative politics.

takes.name: Garry Tan
takes.text:
SF needs to build housing asap

Incentivize and build supply

Stop subsidizing demand

takes.name: Garry Tan
takes.text: NIMBYs like performative feel good virtue signal actions instead of actually solving the real problem by building housing

takes.name: Garry Tan
takes.text:
The founder in their 40s with taste and discernment is the new gentleman unicorn founder

Because there can be 100x to 1000x of them working at their beck and call via agents and software factories all the time

takes.name: Garry Tan
takes.text:
A data center isn't just compute

It's a financing vehicle that pulls power generation, grid, chips, and construction along with it. One buildout drags a dozen adjacent industries forward.

We have to keep building.

stories.title: Bun Keeps Shaving Off Memory and Prepping a Rust Rewrite
stories.summary: Jarred Sumner detailed startup memory cuts and faster Web Streams heading into the next Bun release, plus teased a Rust rewrite blog post and Bun v1.4.

takes.name: Jarred Sumner
takes.text:
In the next version of Bun

Bun uses 8 MB less memory on Linux at start https://t.co/sMQ4xgEKxG

takes.name: Jarred Sumner
takes.text:
rust rewrite blog post Monday 

Bun v1.4 hopefully Tuesday (video still editing)

takes.name: Jarred Sumner
takes.text:
In the next version of Bun

Web Streams get faster, use less memory, and pass 100% of relevant Web Platform Tests https://t.co/pu2Jo1nozD

hotTakes.name: Sam Altman
hotTakes.text: our older kid put two words together for the first time and i am approximately as amazed by this cognitive feat as i am by GPT-5.6 discovering new math

hotTakes.name: vaxry
hotTakes.text:
if billionaires wanted to make the world a better place they'd have sponsored hyprland

alas

hotTakes.name: teej dv 🔭
hotTakes.text: The joke is that the CD is the only reliable way to access your git repository

hotTakes.name: Guillermo Rauch
hotTakes.text:
I like my airplanes like I like my 𝚁𝚎𝚊𝚌𝚝.𝚌𝚛𝚎𝚊𝚝𝚎𝙴𝚕𝚎𝚖𝚎𝚗𝚝 syntax: JSX

A glimmer of hope for how great airtravel could be again.

Lightning quick onboarding, no security theater, Starlink, unstressed friendly staff, convenient terminals. https://t.co/D32Npuxvlg

hotTakes.name: François Chollet
hotTakes.text: The only long term bottlenecks are information and energy.

hotTakes.name: Kent C. Dodds 🏹
hotTakes.text:
The cool thing about what happened in December of 2025 was that models got so good that my ambition for the work they could do took a step-level change.

That's happening again with Fable. My ideas are bigger than they were before.

hotTakes.name: Can Vardar
hotTakes.text: we don’t need AGI to stop developers from reading code. they already don’t read it
